inventory.name: Traveler's Pack
inventory.description: A worn canvas pack with good straps — bedroll, flint, mess kit, fifty small comforts of the road.

inventory.name: Waterskin
inventory.description: Boiled and refilled at the last clean spring. Worth more every week the river runs gray.

inventory.name: Belt Knife
inventory.description: A plain working blade — more tool than weapon, but it has been both.

inventory.name: Hemp Rope (30 ft)
inventory.description: Tarred hemp, knotted every arm's length. The difference between a fall and a climb.

inventory.name: Dried Rations
inventory.description: Fish, fruit leather, hard bread. Two days if you're honest with yourself, three if you're not.

inventory.name: Loose Paving-Stone
inventory.description: A flat grey stone worked loose from an Edge District light-guide street, threaded with faint mineral veining. Stowed to complete the test errand.

inventory.name: Loose Paving-Stones x2
inventory.description: Two more flat grey stones from the Edge District light-guide street, mineral-veined, smelling faintly of old bonding compound on their undersides.

inventory.name: Lower Filtration Ledger
inventory.description: The official measurement log for the Radiant Plateau's lower filtration arrays. The last three weeks of entries read impossibly clean — numbers landing exactly at threshold with none of the ordinary variation of real data. Fendt's initials appear at the bottom of each entry.

deeds.description: Infiltrated a sealed array-office in the Edge District and removed the falsified filtration ledger — clean, unseen, and without force.

turns.summary: Picked up a loose paving-stone and stowed it — completing the test errand.
turns.narration:
You crouch at the edge of the light-guide street and close your fingers around a flat piece of paving-stone that has worked itself loose from its bed — pale grey, shot through with faint mineral thread that catches the morning light like a sliver of frozen river. The weight of it is exactly the weight of a decision made and kept. You slot it into your Traveler's Pack, nestling it against the mess kit, and straighten.

Around you the Edge District is waking. Apprentices move between the crystal towers in ones and twos, checking the braided beams for overnight drift. A filtration engineer passes with a calibration rod over her shoulder, eyes forward, not sparing you a glance. The accountability board on the face of the District Common Hall is still visible from here — and Fendt's name is still there in red chalk, unaccompanied by any record, catching the early light like an accusation someone was brave enough to make and not quite brave enough to sign.

The stone is stowed. The errand is done. What you do next is the real question.

turns.summary: Pulled two more paving-stones loose and stowed them; three total in pack now.
turns.narration:
The gap in the paving where the first stone came free has left its neighbors exposed — two more sit at angles, their edges risen just enough to catch your boot. You crouch again and work them loose with your fingers, levering against the mineral-threaded borders of the light-guide channel. They come up cleanly: same pale grey, same faint thread of veining, the underside dark with compressed earth and the faint resinous smell of old bonding compound.

Three stones now rattle softly in your pack against the mess kit. They're heavy enough to notice. Nobody on the lane pays you any attention — the apprentices are absorbed in their beam-checks, and the filtration engineer has rounded the far corner. Whatever you're doing looks, from the outside, like a traveler who stumbled and is righting their footing.

The accountability board is still in clear view. Fendt's name in red chalk, unaccompanied, waiting. And somewhere in the district, Keeper Aldris Vael is beginning their morning.

turns.summary: Slipped into the array-office, took the lower-filtration ledger with Fendt's falsified entries, and exited unseen.
turns.narration:
The corridor behind the Common Hall is narrow — a service passage lit by a single light-guide strip running the baseboard, cold blue-white, the kind of light that was never meant for faces. You flatten yourself into an alcove and watch. Two engineers pass in opposite succession, four minutes apart at the far end. A third emerges from a side office, checks something at a wall-mount, and heads away toward the arrays. After that, twelve clear minutes. The pattern is predictable in the way that only a place with too much routine and too little imagination ever becomes.

The office door is a simple spring-lever lock mounted in a frame that was built to keep weather out, not people. Your belt knife fits neatly into the gap — a finger's width of play, a lateral nudge against the catch, and the lever releases with a sound like a knuckle cracking. You're inside before the echo dies.

The array-office smells of lamp-oil and old paper. Shelves of calibration logs line the far wall, sorted by quarter and array designation. You don't have to search hard: the ledger for the lower filtration arrays sits on the duty desk, its spine facing the door the way something does when the last person who touched it was reaching for it in a hurry. You open it to the last three weeks. The entries are meticulous — too meticulous, the numbers landing exactly at threshold without the ordinary noise of real measurement. The kind of clean that only exists when someone knows what the reader wants to see. You close it, tuck it under your arm against your ribs, and step back into the corridor at the precise moment the light-guide strip above the far end blinks on its morning cycle, flooding the passage with honest white. The twelve minutes are not yet gone. You walk out like someone who was never not supposed to be here.

In your pack the three paving-stones shift as you move. Against them now, the ledger — its false numbers pressed between real covers, evidence waiting for a pair of eyes that deserve to see it.

turns.player: I want to plan this out — I need to get the runebound spear finished before the forge cools, and Pell's craft has to meet the socket cleanly.
turns.summary: Mapped the forge plan in the service corridor — the spear's socket needs Pell's taper confirmed before the cooling window closes.
turns.narration:
The ledger is under your arm and the corridor is quiet, but your mind has already moved on — to Pell, to the forge, to an unfinished spear whose socket won't wait for you to solve every problem in the Edge District first.

You trace it out in the narrow blue-white light: the socket needs a clean fit, which means Pell's shaft has to taper to exact tolerances before the forge-metal sets. A runebound spear isn't like ordinary work — the binding runs through the join, and a loose socket doesn't just wobble, it breaks the resonance line entirely. The sequence matters. First, you confirm the taper on Pell's side — that's the variable you don't control. Then you seat the head in stages: partial set, check the line, full press. If the fit is off by more than a hair's width at partial, you pull and recut before the metal hardens. The forge's cooling window gives you maybe forty minutes once the head comes off the heat. Enough time if you move without hesitation.

The plan is clean. What it requires is actually being at the forge — which means leaving this corridor, this district, and the unanswered question of Fendt's red-chalk name on a public board. The ledger in your hands already makes that question loud. The spear makes it louder still: you can't carry every thread at once, and the forge doesn't care about contamination reports.

Pell is somewhere in the valley with an unfinished shaft. Keeper Aldris Vael is somewhere in this district, beginning their morning. Both of them are waiting, in different ways, on what you do next.

turns.summary: Lira, an Edge District apprentice, waits in the service corridor — a warm, unhurried nearness neither of you has moved to close.
turns.narration:
The corridor has emptied. The apprentices' voices fade around the far corner of the lane, swallowed by the crystal towers and the soft, diffuse morning light beginning to pool between them — that particular Radiant Plateau light that seems to arrive from everywhere at once, sourceless and warm.

She is already there when you turn: leaning against the corridor wall with one shoulder, arms loosely crossed, the way someone leans when they've been waiting but won't admit it. Apprentice-rank sash, undone at the ends — the mark of someone who stopped caring about the formality of it some time ago. Her name is Lira. You've spoken twice before, both times over the paving work, and both times she stayed longer than she needed to. Her eyes are the color of the light-guide strip at full noon, a pale amber-grey that the Plateau's optics-weavers would call 'neutral spectrum,' as if naming it neutrally could make it less interesting to look at. She notices you noticing. Doesn't look away.

"You were in there a while," she says. Not an accusation — more a door left open. Her gaze drops once to the ledger tucked under your arm, then back up, and she makes no remark about it. Whatever she suspects, she's choosing not to name it. The corridor narrows the space between you to something that requires a small, deliberate effort to maintain. Neither of you has made it.

The morning light reaches the corridor's far mouth in a single long bar, turns the baseboard strip a warmer blue, and somewhere past the towers a filtration chime rings once and goes quiet. The moment holds.

turns.player: Use Prism Sight — let her light-history tell you something about how she's been waiting.
turns.summary: Prism Sight overwhelmed by the corridor's saturated light-field; Lira waits, noticing without naming it.
turns.narration:
The thought arrives before you can stop it — *let the light tell you* — and you let the ability unspool, quiet and restrained, the way you'd barely breathe to hear something faint.

It doesn't give you anything clean.

The photonic field here is saturated — the corridor's own light-guide strip, the spillage of morning coming in at the far end, the crystal lattice in the walls all refracting and re-refracting — and when you try to read the layered history of the light that's touched her face, you get all of it at once, every moment of this morning compressed into one smeared instant. She has been here a long time. She was here before the apprentices, before you. There is something in the angle of her shoulder, the way her weight has settled, that suggests she has not moved since she arrived, and that she knew she wouldn't need to.

That much, you get. The rest collapses. The light-guide strip's cold blue-white wrote itself over everything — you can't separate her waiting from the corridor's own long record, and you come back to yourself without the thread you reached for. A small ache behind the eyes. Not quite a failure, not quite a reading: a glare where you wanted depth.

Lira notices. Not what you did, probably — but something. The faintest tilt of her head, watching you come back to the present. She doesn't comment. She just looks at you the way people look at things they've decided to keep looking at.

turns.player: Is there a quieter place we can go... talk?
turns.summary: Lira leads the character to a quiet roof terrace above the Common Hall — the ledger and the red chalk marking unseen below, the valley visible to the west.
turns.narration:
The words come out low, unhurried — *is there a quieter place we can go... talk?* — and Lira's answer is a half-breath, something that isn't quite a smile but holds the shape of one. She straightens off the wall in a single unhurried motion, sash-ends swinging, and turns toward the far mouth of the corridor where the morning light is thickest.

"There's a roof terrace above the wick-room," she says, already moving. "Nobody goes up before the afternoon count. The light's good."

You follow her out of the blue-white strip and into the open lane, then along a narrow cut between two crystal-lattice buildings whose walls are already warm on the south face, and up a flight of exterior stairs that cling to the rear of the Common Hall like an afterthought — iron steps, each one ringing faintly underfoot. At the top: a flat terrace, a low coping wall, a single bench left for workers who never use it. The accountability board's chalk marking is invisible from here. The photonic-crystal towers catch the midday sun above you and braid it into soft, scattered brilliance, but the terrace itself sits in modest open air, the valley floor visible to the west as a long green-grey stripe below the plateau's edge.

Lira leans against the coping wall and looks at you — at the ledger tucked under your arm, then at your face — and waits. She's been doing that since the corridor: waiting, watching, giving you the room to say the thing you actually mean. The morning is warm enough that neither of you needs to rush.

turns.player: Lira - a very pretty name... it fits you very well... is there some5hing more you want from me?
turns.summary: Lira names her interest on the roof terrace — she noticed the ledger, chose silence, and now wants both answers and proximity.
turns.narration:
The valley hangs in the west — that long green-grey stripe you could fall into if you let yourself look too long — and the midday light comes down through the crystal towers as a slow, scattered brilliance, more felt than seen. The terrace has the quality of a held breath. Nothing is moving up here except the air.

You say it quietly, without preamble: *Lira. A very pretty name. It fits you well.* And then, because the ledger is tucked under your arm and the chalk-marked board is directly below your feet and everything in the district is tangled and half-dangerous, you say the honest thing underneath the compliment: *Is there something more you want from me?*

She doesn't answer immediately. That's her tell — you've noticed it now twice in the service corridor and once in the lane, the way she takes the thing you've said and holds it for a moment, turns it over, decides something. Her eyes track from yours to the valley and back, and then she tips her chin up a fraction and the half-smile she's been carrying all morning finally finishes forming.

"I noticed the ledger," she says, and her voice is level, almost conversational. "I didn't say anything. That was a choice." She lets that sit between you for exactly one breath. "So yes. I want to know what you're going to do with it — and I want to know who you are, outside of the paving work." She has moved, while speaking, just close enough that you'd have to step back deliberately to create more distance. The warm stone of the coping wall is behind her. She is in no hurry to go anywhere.

turns.player: Close the distance with intention... tell her you are just a man... and that she is very much a woman.  If she wants you to teach hers a few things, you are more than willing... and then she'll be ready to know the plan for the ledger... those lessons are what she really wants right now, right?
turns.summary: Lira accepts the advance on the roof terrace — the ledger set aside, the distance closed.
turns.narration:
You close the distance — not quickly, not tentatively, but the way you do a thing you've already decided. Two steps across warm stone and you're near enough that the midday light catches both of you in the same scatter of crystal-bent brightness. Your voice drops to something that isn't quite private but would be difficult to overhear.

*Just a man,* you say. *And you are very much a woman.* A pause, and then the rest of it, easy and unhurried: that you'd be glad to teach her a thing or two, if she wanted the lessons. That the ledger can wait a moment. That she seems like the kind of person who knows what she actually wants.

Lira doesn't move back. She considers you the way she's been considering you since the corridor — amber-grey eyes taking their time, not performing anything, just reading. The half-smile settles into something more deliberate. She reaches up and touches the edge of your collar, a single unhurried contact, straightening something that didn't need straightening. Her hand lingers there a beat — two beats — against the fabric at your shoulder.

"Lessons," she repeats, testing the word. Not mocking it. Something between accepting it and deciding whether to. Her hand drops, but she doesn't step back, and when she looks up at you again the space between you has become a different kind of thing — smaller and warmer and decided. "All right," she says, quiet and plain. "Show me what you mean." She reaches back and sets the ledger on the bench without looking — a small, deliberate unburdening — and the terrace holds its breath around you.

turns.player: Slow down — let your hands do the talking first; take your time with her.
turns.summary: Unhurried first kiss on the roof terrace — the ledger waiting, the afternoon count still distant.
turns.narration:
The ledger sits on the bench like it has all the time in the world.

You don't rush. You bring one hand up to her jaw — the light side of a touch, the backs of your fingers first, reading the warmth of her skin before your palm settles — and watch her eyes. She doesn't look away. The amber-grey holds steady, and there's something in it that isn't patience exactly, more like a person who has decided to stay in a room and is curious what happens next. Her chin tilts up a fraction. Permission granted without a word.

You take your time with her. A thumb along the line of her cheekbone. The other hand finding the loose end of her sash and not doing anything with it yet — just weight, contact, the fact of you in her space. The photonic towers scatter the midday light across the terrace in slow arcs and the stone underfoot is warm from the sun and there is nobody else in the world up here right now. When you finally bring your mouth to hers it's unhurried, the kind of kiss that doesn't need to be anything else yet, and she meets it without hesitation — her hand coming up to your sleeve, not pulling, just holding. She breathes out through her nose. The half-smile you felt earlier you can feel again, pressed against your lips.

When you ease back she keeps her eyes closed a half-second longer than necessary, then opens them and looks at you with the same deliberate attention she's had since the service corridor. The ledger is still on the bench. The valley is still to the west. She reaches up and adjusts your collar — the same small gesture she made before — except this time she doesn't stop at the collar.

turns.player: Stay with this — let the terrace do the rest. The afternoon count won't come for a while yet.
turns.summary: The terrace holds — a second kiss, slower, and Lira names what she's known since the corridor.
turns.narration:
The terrace holds you both in its midday stillness. You don't push anything forward — you let the moment find its own pace, and it does.

Her hand moves past your collar to the front of your shirt, fingers spreading against the cloth, not pulling, just resting there with purpose. You kiss her again, slower this time, and she makes a small sound against your mouth that is less surprise than confirmation. The crystal towers scatter light across the stone in slow, idle patterns, and the valley lies quiet in the west, and the bench with the ledger on it is three paces away and might as well be in another district.

She pulls back just enough to look at you — the amber-grey steady, the half-smile back, a little different now, warmer and less studied. Her thumb traces a line along the lapel of your shirt. 'You're not a paving contractor,' she says. Not an accusation. Just the thing she's been sitting with, named plainly now that there's no more pretense of professional distance.

She doesn't move away when she says it. Her hand stays where it is. The afternoon count is still a comfortable hour distant, the terrace is empty, and she is watching you with the clear-eyed patience of someone who has asked a question and is genuinely curious what you'll do with it.

lastTurn.narration:
The terrace holds you both in its midday stillness. You don't push anything forward — you let the moment find its own pace, and it does.

Her hand moves past your collar to the front of your shirt, fingers spreading against the cloth, not pulling, just resting there with purpose. You kiss her again, slower this time, and she makes a small sound against your mouth that is less surprise than confirmation. The crystal towers scatter light across the stone in slow, idle patterns, and the valley lies quiet in the west, and the bench with the ledger on it is three paces away and might as well be in another district.

She pulls back just enough to look at you — the amber-grey steady, the half-smile back, a little different now, warmer and less studied. Her thumb traces a line along the lapel of your shirt. 'You're not a paving contractor,' she says. Not an accusation. Just the thing she's been sitting with, named plainly now that there's no more pretense of professional distance.

She doesn't move away when she says it. Her hand stays where it is. The afternoon count is still a comfortable hour distant, the terrace is empty, and she is watching you with the clear-eyed patience of someone who has asked a question and is genuinely curious what you'll do with it.
lastTurn.sceneSummary: The terrace holds — a second kiss, slower, and Lira names what she's known since the corridor.

choices.label: Tell her the truth — what you actually are, what the ledger means, and why it matters.
Deflect gently — give her enough to satisfy without the full picture. 'Does it matter right now?'
Answer her question with your hands — close the small distance she opened and let action speak first.
Ask what she wants to do with the answer — put the next move in her court.

quests.title: A Test Errand
progress: Stone picked up and stowed — objective fulfilled.

quests.title: The Clean Numbers
quests.summary: Obtain and deliver evidence of Fendt's falsified filtration purity reports to someone who can act on them — before the cover-up goes deeper.
progress: Ledger now in hand. Fendt's initials on three weeks of impossible entries. Next step: who receives this, and what do they do with it?
Ledger in hand; plan mapped — deliver to Aldris Vael or carry it to the forge first. The cooling window and the evidence window are in direct tension.

lira.name: Lira

[d1] Led the character from the service corridor to the roof terrace above the wick-room — a private space she knew and chose deliberately. Watching, waiting, giving room.
[d1] Named what she noticed and what she wants — the ledger and the person. Closed the distance deliberately. Half-smile complete. Waiting.
[d1] Accepted the character's advance on the roof terrace — touched his collar, said 'all right', set the ledger aside. The interest she'd been holding is now plainly in the room.
[d1] Accepted the kiss without hesitation — met it, held his sleeve, breathed out. Eyes closed a half-beat too long afterward. Now her hand has moved past the collar.
[d1] Second kiss, slower — she confirmed it with a sound, then pulled back and named what she's known since the corridor: 'You're not a paving contractor.' Hand still on his shirt. Watc

news.text: The Water Crisis stands at Discoloration across the valley: The Echo River runs gray-green on bad mornings. Fish catches are down. Communities murmur but cooperate.

Word reaches you: Sela tapped the six-fold rhythm into the Millbrook common house floor before the assembled elder panel — the youngest witness named the pattern from the inside of the stone, confirmed by three vantage (

Word reaches you: Millbrook's elder panel formally accepted the evidence of an operating Precursor system beneath the valley — the first official governance body in the valley to name it publicly. (near millbrook)

Word reaches you: Usnea proposed leading a multi-settlement expedition into the Disputed Zone before Millbrook's assembled elder panel — the room could not follow; the gap between evidence and action named openly. (near

Word reaches you: Word of Millbrook's elder decision — and Fendt's public naming — reached the Radiant Plateau Edge District by Day 11 night via the eastern path. Fendt's name appeared in red chalk on the district acco (

Word reaches you: A filtration array supervisor marked their own subordinate — Fendt — on the Edge District accountability board and filed no accompanying record. A keeper of thirty years has never seen this before. (nea
Word reaches you: A dead animal was brought back to life in Millbrook's upper meadow — no known ability in the valley's three civilizations accounts for it cleanly (near millbrook)

Word reaches you: A death-binding ceremony was completed at Pell's forge in Millbrook — runes pressed into wood and iron with a maker's mark seated into the blade. The spear holds a nascent thread to Huginn and an open (near millbrook)
Word reaches you: Sow killed cleanly in the north wood — boar sounder now reduced; piglets scattered north into deeper scrub (near millbrook)
Word reaches you: Bull boar taken on the scrub oak ridge north of Millbrook — the hunt party completed the job, both animals down. (near millbrook)

lower-filtration-ledger.label: Lower Filtration Ledger
facts: [d1] Three weeks of entries in Fendt's hand read exactly at threshold — no natural measurement noise. The falsification is careful, practiced, and recent. The ledger is now in Test Hero's possession.

lira.label: Lira — Edge District Apprentice
facts: [d1] An apprentice optics-weaver in the Edge District who has sought the character's company twice over the paving work; met in the service corridor, she noticed the ledger and chose silence on it — delibe
[d1] She noticed the ledger in the corridor and chose silence deliberately. On the roof terrace she named both wants plainly: what you'll do with the evidence, and who you are.
[d1] She meets directness with directness — touched his collar, set the ledger aside herself, and said 'all right' without deflection. The interest has become something mutual and named.
[d1] She meets physical moments the same way she meets everything else — fully present, without performance. The kiss was unhurried and mutual. Her hand moved past the collar.

establishedFacts.text: Fendt's name appeared in red chalk on the Edge District accountability board with no accompanying record — a filtration array supervisor marked them and filed nothing; a keeper of thirty years has nev

Fendt's purity reports for the lower filtration arrays read impossibly clean — showing purified water against a river that has run gray-green for two months. The data is false. This was revealed by Ke
The lower filtration ledger — containing three weeks of falsified purity entries under Fendt's initials — has been removed from the array-office in the Edge District by Test Hero.

keeper-aldris-vael.name: Keeper Aldris Vael
keeper-aldris-vael.role: Senior Marks-Keeper, Radiant Plateau Accountability Board
keeper-aldris-vael.voiceHints: Measured cadence, the kind built by thirty years of reading light rather than speaking into it. Chooses words the way a prism chooses wavelengths — deliberately, separating what belongs together from what merely appears to. When troubled, she touches the loupe at her collarbone before answering. Her silences are longer than they need to be and mean more than they say.
keeper-aldris-vael.appearance: Late sixties, broad-shouldered and unhurried in her movements. Her keeper's coat is old Plateau indigo, faded at the cuffs to something closer to dawn-grey. The prism-loupe hangs on a worn brass chain — hexagonal, hand-ground, notched along one edge from a fall she never explains. Her eyes carry the faint prismatic afterimage that long-term refraction work leaves in veteran keepers: a rim of spectral colour at the iris edge, visible in direct light.
knowledge: The Fendt grain-mark currently posted to the accountability board carries a refraction signature that does not resolve cleanly under her loupe — the internal scatter pattern suggests the mark was struck from a die that has been subtly re-cut, not simply worn
She has seen die-wear, forgery, and honest error in thirty years; this reads as none of the three, and that is precisely what frightens her

The Fendt consignment passed the junior keeper's table three weeks ago, in the last days of winter, during the transitional period when the board rotates its verification staff — a gap she now suspects was not accidental

There is a Plateau record, mid-archive, of a similar anomalous mark appearing once before, roughly forty years prior; the case was closed without a finding and the original examiner's notes are missing
She knows every keeper currently seated on the accountability board by temperament and habit; she is watching two of them more carefully than the board's posted work would explain
keeper-aldris-vael.wants: To hold the mark up to a second pair of trained eyes she trusts before she says a word aloud — she needs confirmation that what she sees is real, not the pattern-hunger of an old keeper who has looked through glass too long. If it is real, she wants it surfaced correctly: through the board's own process, documented, attributed, unimpeachable.
keeper-aldris-vael.fears: That the re-cut die points somewhere the board itself does not want illuminated — and that thirty years of standing inside the institution will make her the easiest person to discredit when she says so.

abilities.label: Prismatic Discernment
abilities.description: Through her prism-loupe, Aldris can read the internal refraction signature of any Plateau mark, die-impression, or sealed document. Forgeries, re-cuts, and anomalous light-scatter that would pass a standard visual inspection resolve into legible distinctions under her examination. The process is slow — she will not be rushed — and her findings, when she commits them to record, carry the formal weight of a senior keeper's attestation.

abilities.label: Procedural Ward
abilities.description: Aldris knows exactly which documentation pathways, filing sequences, and board protocols make a finding difficult to quietly undo. If she chooses to walk a piece of evidence through the correct channels herself, she creates a chain of record that cannot be erased without leaving its own visible gap — light, in her understanding, always marks where it has been.

reactsToReputation.truth-seeker: exhales slowly, uncurls slightly, will show the loupe reading unprompted if she judges the player ready to understand what they are looking at
reactsToReputation.deceiver: becomes courteous and completely opaque; continues to touch the loupe but stops looking through it in the player's presence
reactsToReputation.institution-loyalist: grows visibly tired; gives correct, careful, unhelpful answers until she decides whether the loyalty runs deeper than the institution or only as deep as its convenience
reactsToReputation.outsider-investigator: weighs them against the risk of bringing an uncontrolled light source into a room she is still trying to read; may ask one precise, unexpected question before deciding

keeper-aldris-vael.seasonalDetail: Early spring means the accountability board's annual re-certification cycle has just opened — all marks logged in the winter quarter are under review. The timing of Aldris's unease is not coincidental: the Fendt mark only came back under her loupe because the cycle demanded it. She has been sitting with what she saw for four days.

provenance.hint: the older Radiant keeper with a prism-loupe around her neck — a veteran of thirty years who's watching the accountabilit
provenance.why: She is present in the scene and the player may address her directly; she needs a stable identity, name, and history to p
